Facts.text: An earthquake strikes Constantinople, causing damage to city walls and buildings
Comet 55P/1366 U1 (Tempel-Tuttle) approaches within 0.0229 astronomical units of Earth
Amsterdam buccaneer Herman of Kuinre signs loyalty vow for peace
Mobs attack Jewish community of Kraków
Lead (graphite) pencils first used
Spanish troops give Milan to France
Arung Palakka's Bugis forces occupy Makassar, southern Sulawesi
Georgia Colony reverses itself & rules slavery is legal
First Continental Congress adjourns in Philadelphia
Minutemen organized in US colonies
The Directoire, a five-man revolutionary government of France, is created
King Willem I requires inhabitants of Brussels to use Dutch language
Erie Canal between Hudson River & Lake Erie opens
Belgian rebels occupy Antwerp
Hamilton Smith patents rotary washing machine in Pittsburgh

Steam clipper Royal Charter is wrecked on the coast of Anglesey, Wales;, in a storm that took down over 100 vessels; approximately 450 passengers and crew killed on voyage returning to Liverpool from Australia
Football Association forms in England, standardizing soccer and splitting from rugby
International conference begins in Geneva aimed at improving medical conditions on battlefields, beginning of the Red Cross
White terrorists kill several blacks in St Bernard Parish La
1st American steeplechase horserace at Westchester, NY
South Carolina Governor Chamberlain sends a company of federal troops to quell racial disturbances in Cainhoy
Abyssinia & Italy sign peace treaty
First recorded use of a "getaway car" occurs after a shop is held up in Paris
Yerba Buena is first Key System ferry to cross San Francisco Bay
First Soviet (workers' council) formed, St Petersburg, Russia
Sweden recognises Norway as an independent constitutional monarchy, effectively dissolving the United Kingdoms of Sweden and Norway
Serbian troops over run Skopje (Uskup)
Woolwich Foot Tunnel under the Thames river in England opens
Petrograd Soviet accepts establishment of Military
World War I: Brazil declares it is in a state of war with the Central Powers
Soldiers revolt at Harskamp, Veluwe
B C Hilliam's musical "Buddies" premieres in NYC
Solomon Porter Hood named US minister to Liberia
Dutch Government of Ruijs de Beerenbrouck resigns
Dutch second Chamber rejects Fleet laws (50-49 vote)
Arthur Goodrich's play "Caponsacchi" premieres in NYC
Belgium stabilizes current value of franc (5 franc becomes 1 "Belga")
French government of Albert Sarraut forms
Polish Jews forced into obligatory work service
RMS Empress is disabled by a German bomb off Ireland's west coast - sunk two days later allowing most passengers to survive
The P-51 Mustang makes its maiden flight.
Battle of Santa Cruz: Japanese naval offensive against US forces near Solomon Islands
Fourth day of battle at El Alamein: Australian breakthrough
Second day of Battle of Henderson Field, Guadalcanal
World War II: First flight of the Dornier Do 335 "Pfeil".
Maharajah of Jammu & Kashmir accedes to India
The British military occupation ends in Iraq
630 Dutch volunteers depart for Korea
South Korean troops reach Chosan at Chinese boundary
Emile Zatopek runs world record 30,000m, 25,000m & 15 miles
Great Britain performs nuclear test at Emu Field, Australia
Chevrolet unveils V-8 engine
City of Trieste returned to Italy
British troops occupy Saudi Arabian oil field at Boeraimi
First edition of "Village Voice" (NYC) published
UN's International Atomic Energy Agency statute approved
Vietnam promulgates its constitution
PanAm flies first transatlantic jet from New York to Paris
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site

MLB: American League approves existing Washington Senators move to become Minnesota Twins, and two new expansion franchises - Los Angeles Angels & Washington (D.C.) Senators; 1961 season schedule grows from 154 to 162 games
First test flight of Saturn launch vehicle
US performs atmospheric nuclear test at Johnston Island
US performs underground nuclear test at Fallon, Nevada
Eric Edgar Cooke becomes last person in Western Australia to be executed.
Queen Elizabeth decorates The Beatles with medals making them members Order of the British Empire (MBE) at Buckingham Palace
Sylvia Likens is tortured and murdered by her caregiver, Gertrude Baniszewski, Baniszewski's children, and several neighborhood members (torture lasts three months)
First Pacific communications satellite launched, Intelsat 2
US aircraft carrier Oriskany catches fire at Gulf on Tonken, 43 die
Charlie Hickcox wins his 3rd gold medal of the Mexico City Olympics when he leads the US men's 4 x 100m medley relay team with teammates Don McKenzie, Doug Russell & Ken Walsh to world record 3:54.9
First European satellite launched, Esro 1, at Cape Kennedy
Italian diver Klaus Dibiasi wins first of 3 consecutive men's 10m platform gold medals when he comfortably beats Álvaro Gaxiola of Mexico at the Mexico City Olympics
Japanese gymnast Akinori Nakayama wins 3 individual gold medals on the one day (horizontal bar, rings & parallel bars) at the Mexico City Olympics; with team victory, 4 gold for the Games
Japanese gymnasts sweep the medals in the men's floor exercise competition at the Mexico City Olympics; Sawao Kato wins his 3rd gold of the Games ahead of teammates Akinori Nakayama and Takeshi Katō
KMIR TV channel 36 in Palm Springs, CA (NBC) begins broadcasting
Soviet Union launches spacecraft Soyuz 3
Striker Antal Dunai scores twice as Hungary outclasses Bulgaria 4-1 to take the men's football gold medal at the Mexico City Olympics
The Soviet Union wraps up the volleyball double at the Mexico City Olympics; both men's & women's teams take gold at the end of their respective round-robin competitions
WHMA (now WJSU) TV channel 40 in Anniston, AL (CBS) 1st broadcast
"Doonesbury" comic strip debuts in 28 newspapers
Tanzania begins building railway Lusaka-Drone ash Salaam
An Assembly, attended only by Nationalist politicians, and acting as an alternative to Stormont, meet in Dungiven Castle
UN votes to replace Taiwan with China
Israeli forces reach Suez, trapping Egyptian army
President Nixon releases first White House tapes on Watergate scandal
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Wings release single "Helen Wheels"
Cleveland Coliseum opens for NBA's Cavaliers & MISL's Crunch
China performs nuclear test at Lop Nor, PRC
Cuba beats Mexico for its 4th Pan Am Games Gold Medal in baseball
Transkei gains independence, not recognized outside of South Africa
Trinidad & Tobago becomes a republic
5th & final test of space shuttle Enterprise
Dr Clifford R. Wharton Jr. named chancellor of State University of New York
Last natural case of smallpox is discovered in Merca District, Somalia, considered the anniversary of the eradication of smallpox, the most spectacular success of vaccination
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Independent Counsel Act is signed into law.
Menachem Begin andAnwar Sadatnamed joint winners of 1978 Nobel Peace Prize
Park Chung-hee, President of South Korea is assassinated by KCIA head Kim Jae-kyu. Choi Kyu-ha becomes the acting President; Kim is executed the following May.
St Louis Cardinals sack Baltimore Colt QBs an NFL record tying 12 times
Hugh Whitemore's "Pack of Lies" premieres in London
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Stephanie Fae Beauclair ("Baby Fae") gets baboon heart transplant, lives 21 days

CBS' premiere of fact based TV film "Children of the Night", based on sociology student Lois Lee's
expose on female crime and inconsistent enforcement of prostitution laws in Los Angeles
Hurricane Juan kills 97 in US
Dow Jones Industrial Average down 156.83 points
Head of Salvadoran Human Rights Comm assassinated by death squads
US-Soviet effort to free 2 grey whales from frozen Arctic, Barrow, AK
London Ambulance Service thrown into chaos after implementation problems with a new Computer Aided Dispatch (CAD) system
The Charlottetown Accord fails to win majority support in a Canada wide referendum.
NFL announces new expansion team, Carolina Panthers in Charlotte
NJ Devils lose 2-0 to Montreal, after winning 1st 7 games of 1993
Jordan & Israel sign peace accord
Israeli-Palestinian Conflict: Mossad agents assassinate Islamic Jihad leader Fathi Shikaki in his hotel in Malta.
Britain's House of Lords votes to end the right of hereditary peers to vote in Britain's upper chamber of Parliament.
Laurent Gbagbo takes over as president of Côte d'Ivoire following a popular uprising against President Robert Guéï
The United States passes the USA PATRIOT Act into law.

Moscow Theatre Siege ends: Approximately 50 Chechen rebels and 150 hostages die when Russian Spetsnaz storm a theater building in Moscow, which had been occupied by the rebels during a musical performance three days before
The Cedar Fire, the second-largest fire in Californian history, kills 15 people, consumes 250,000 acres (1,000 km²), destroys 2,200 homes around San Diego

Argentine intelligence officer Alfredo Astiz, known as "The Blonde Angel of Death" and others are jailed for life for crimes against humanity, including the deaths of the founders of the Mothers of the Plaza de Mayo
41 people are killed and 50 injured by a suicide bombing of a mosque in Maymana, Afghanistan
64 people are killed in West Burma after continued sectarian clashes
China blocks the New York Times from searches and social media in response to an investigation into Premier Wen Jiabao
Hurricane Sandy makes landfall in the Bahamas killing 2 people and causing over $300 million in damage
7.5 magnitude earthquake hits northern Pakistan and Afghanistan killing over 300
World Health Organization classifies processed meat as carcinogenic
Jesus' supposed tomb is opened for the first time in 500 years by archaeologists for restoration in the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, Jerusalem
Explosion and fire at a fireworks factory in Tangerang, Indonesia kills 47 and injuries dozens
Oldest-known tsunami victim revealed by sediment discovered in 6,000 year-old skull by scientists near Aitape, Papua New Guinea
Rhine River in drought with lowest levels since 1920s forces German government to release oil reserves after barge shipments disrupted
Trump supporter Cesar Sayoc arrested for sending 14 pipe bombs to prominent US Democrats
Raid by US Special Forces kills ISIS founder Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi in Syria
Melbourne officials announce end to their three-month lockdown as city records no new cases of COVID-19 for 1st time since June
NASA announces there is more water on the Moon than previously thought, in sunlit as well as shadowed regions
Pakistan's first metro line, the Orange Line, opens in Lahore
Police officers in Philadelphia shoot and kill black man Walter Wallace Jr. armed with a knife, prompting protests and the city to impose a curfew
Silverado wildfire breaks out in Orange County, California forcing 60,000 people to evacuate
US Senate confirms Amy Coney Barrett to the Supreme Court sealing a conservative 6-3 majority
UN report says current climate pledges put world on course for "catastrophic" average 2.7-degree Celsius temperature rise this century ahead of Glasgow climate summit
European Union's tallest building completed - the Varso Tower, a supertall skyscraper in Warsaw, Poland, at 310 metres high
Thousands of protesters in Saqez, Iran defy security forces to mark 40-day Arba'een (period of mourning) for Mahsa Amini, a 22-year old who died in police custody for not wearing a hijab properly
Israel carries out a series of airstrikes on Iranian military targets in and around Tehran
Thomas Moreappointed Lord Chancellor of England
Charles Vnames George van Egmond (Joris van Egmont) as Bishop of Utrecht
Prince William of Orangeoccupies Grave after a siege
William Pennaccepts area around Delaware River from Duke of York
Benjamin Franklindeparts for France on a mission to seek French support for the American Revolution

First presidential election in American history where the popular vote is important with 18 states choosing presidential electors by popular vote.Andrew Jacksonwins the most votes but is short of a majority, forcing a contingent election in the House of Representatives whichJohn Quincy Adamswins.
Robert McCluresights the fabled Northwest Passage for the first time, from Banks Island towards Melville Island
Union troops ambush and kill BillWilliam T. Andersonknown as "Bloody Bill" near Albany, Missouri
Anton Brucknerleads the Vienna Philharmonic in the premiere of his "Symphony No. 2"
"Southern Horrors: Lynch Law in All Its Phases" first published by African American journalistIda B. Wellsin Memphis, Tennessee
German EmperorWilhelm IIfires chancellor Leo von Caprivi and Prime Minister of Prussia Botho zu Eulenburg
Itō Hirobumi, Resident-General of Korea, and former Japanese Prime Minister is shot and killed by Korean nationalist An Jung-geun in Harbin, China
DictatorVictoriano Huertaelected president of Mexico
American sex educatorMargaret Sangerarrested for obscenity (advocating birth control)
Edward Elgar's Cello Concerto in E minor, Op. 85, his last notable work, premieres at Queen's Hall, London
US PresidentWoodrow Wilson's veto of Prohibition Enforcement Bill is overridden
Gertrude Bellappointed Honorary Director of Antiquities in new Department of Antiquities in Baghdad, Iraq (origin of the Baghdad Archaeological Museum)
Italian government resigns under pressure from fascists led byBenito Mussolini
American jazz vocalist Adelaide Hall makes her 1st recordings - "Creole Love Call" and "Blues I Love to Sing" - with theDuke EllingtonOrchestra, for Victor Records in Camden, New Jersey
Dmitri Shostakovich's ballet "Zolotoy Vyek" premieres in Leningrad
Eugene O'Neill's play cycle "Mourning Becomes Electra" premieres in NYC
While Washington Senators player-managerJoe Croninhoneymoons with Mildred Robertson, ownerClark Griffith's niece and adopted daughter, he is sold to Red Sox
Second meeting of partisansTitoandDraža Mihailovićin Yugoslavia
US PresidentHarry Trumanincreases minimum wage from 40 cents to 75 cents
Branch Rickeyresigns as Brooklyn Dodgers president
Mother Teresafounds Missionaries of Charity in Calcutta, India
Future world heavyweight boxing championRocky Marcianodefeats former championJoe Louisby TKO in the 8th round at Madison Square Garden
Winston Churchillre-elected British Prime Minister at the age of 76
Ngô Đình Diệmproclaims Vietnam a republic with himself as President
USSR fires defense minister Marshal GeorgiZhukov

Nikita Khrushchevsends note toJFKoffering to withdraw his missiles from Cuba if US closes its bases in Turkey. Offer is rejected and JFK warns Russia that the USA will not allow Soviet missiles to remain in Cuba.
Shah of IranMohammad Reza Pahlavicrowns himself after 26 years on the Peacock Throne
Future world heavyweight boxing championGeorge Foremanwins the Olympic heavyweight gold medal when the final against Jonas Čepulis (Soviet Union) is stopped in round 2 at the Mexico City Games
Edwin Landintroduces the first truly instant camera thePolaroidSX-70 camera at an event in Miami, Florida
Henry Kissingerdeclares "Peace is at hand" in Vietnam
Ringo Starrand singer Lulu appear in non-speaking cameos on "Monty Python's Flying Circus" program
Anwar Sadatbecomes the 1st Egyptian president to officially visit the United States
British rock band Queen andDavid Bowierelease their serendipitous collaborativesingle "Under Pressure"
MLB Philadelphia PhilliesSteve Carltonbecomes 1st pitcher to win 4 Cy Young awards
US budget deficit reaches then record $110.7 billion for fiscal year 1982, the first full year of theRonald Reagan's presidency
"The Terminator" directed byJames Cameron, starringArnold Schwarzenegger,Linda HamiltonandMichael Biehnis released in the US
Doug Harvey's #2 jersey is retired by the Montreal Canadiens
French McLaren driverAlain Prostretains his Formula 1 World Drivers Championship with victory in season ending Australian Grand Prix in Adelaide; wins title by 2 points fromNigel Mansell
Jacques Villeneuve finishes 3rd in European Grand Prix at Jerez, Spain; first Canadian to win F1 World Drivers Championship; wins by 39 points fromMichael Schumacher
American tennis starSerena Williamsbeats Simona Halep of Romania 6–3, 6–0 to successfully defend her WTA Finals title in Kallang, Singapore
Dilma Rousseffis re-elected President of Brazil
"Spectre", 24th James Bond film, directed bySam Mendesand starringDaniel Craigpremieres in London
Rashid Khanmakes his Twenty20 International (T20I) debut for Afghanistan against Zimbabwe
Cremation ceremony for KingBhumibol Adulyadejof Thailand held in Bangkok, a year after his death
Jacinda Ardernis sworn in as Prime Minister of New Zealand, becoming the world’s youngest female head of government
National Party of New Zealand found guilty of breaching the copyright of rapperEminem's publisher and ordered to pay $413,000 for use of the song “Eminem Esque”
US PresidentDonald Trumpdeclares the opioid crisis a public health emergency
Irish singer Sinéad O'Connor announces she has converted to Islam
Japanese Prime MinisterYoshihide Sugasays the country will becomes carbon neutral by 2050
"Black Panther: Wakanda Forever" has its world premiere in Hollywood starring Letitia Wright, Lupita Nyong'o,Danai Gurira
Pony Express (Missouri to California) ends after 19 months
Gunfight at the O.K. Corral: The most famous shootout in the Wild West occurs between lawmen, includingWyatt Earp, and the Cowboys, with Tom and Frank McLaury and Billy Clanton killed
Cecil Chubb gives prehistoric monument Stonehenge to the British nation
Germany's supreme commander General Eric Ludendorff resigns, protesting the terms to which the German Government has agreed in negotiating an armistice
Guided tours of the former prison at Alcatraz by the National Park Service begin
Sigismund, Archduke of Austria, born in Innsbruck, Austria (d. 1496)
Duke Friedrich of Saxony, 36th Grand Master of the Teutonic Knights (1498-1510), born in Torgau, Germany (d. 1510)
Hans Buchner, German composer, born in the Free Imperial City of Ravensburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1538)
Zhengde, 10th Emperor of the Ming Dynasty, born in Beijing, China (d. 1521)

William Sprague, English co-founder of Charlestown, Massachusetts, born in Upwey, Dorset, England (d. 1675)
Aert de Gelder, Dutch painter (King David), born in Dordrecht, Netherlands (d. 1727)

Kurt Christoph Graf von Schwerin, Prussian field marshal, born in Löwitz, Pomerania (d. 1757)
Domenico Scarlatti, Italian composer and harpsichordist (La Silvia), born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples, Spanish Empire (d. 1757)

Ernest Louis Muller, Polish composer and flautist, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1811)

Joseph Mayseder, Austrian violinist and composer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1863)
Charles Sprague, American banker and poet (Curiosity), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1875)
Konstantin Thon, Russian architect (Grand Kremlin Palace), born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1881)
Nikolaos Mantzaros, Italian-Greek composer, born in Corfu Island, Greece (d. 1872)

Henry Smart, English organist and composer, born in London (d. 1879)
Stefano Golinelli, Italian pianist and composer, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1891)
Karl Weinhold, German sociologist (founder of Journal of the Association of Folklore), born in Dzierżoniów, Poland (d. 1901)
Arthur Middleton Manigault, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Charleston, South Carolina (d. 1886)
Polibo Fumagalli, Italian organist, pianist, and composer, born in Inzago, Kingdom of Lombardy–Venetia, Austrian Empire (d. 1900)
Stephen Elliott, Jr., Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Beaufort, South Carolina (d. 1866)
James Lawlor Kieran, Irish-American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Mountbellew, County Galway (d. 1869)
Vasily Vereshchagin, Russian painter (War & Peace), born in Cherepovets, Russia (d. 1904)
Hendrik Waelput, Flemish composer and conductor (Forest, Stella), born in Ghent, Belgium (d. 1885)
Ferdinand Georg Frobenius, German mathematician (Frobenius–Stickelberger formulae), born in Charlotteburg, Berlin, Germany (d. 1917)
August Kiehl, Dutch actor (Op hoop van zegen, De kribbebijter), born in Amsterdam, Noord-Holland, Netherlands (d. 1938)
C. W. Post, American manufacturer of breakfast cereals, born in Springfield, Illinois (d. 1914)
Arthur Friedheim, Russian composer, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1932)
Frank Selee, American Baseball HOF manager (5 x NL C'ship Boston Beaneaters; Chicago Orphans/Cubs), born in Amherst, New Hampshire (d. 1909)
Joseph Moorat, British composer (Prunella), born in England (d. 1938)
Benjamin Guggenheim, American businessman, prominent passenger aboard the RMS Titanic, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1912)
William "Kid" Gleason, American baseball utility (St. Louis Browns, NY Giants, Philadelphia Phillies) and manager (Chicago White Sox, during "Black Sox" scandal), born in Camden, New Jersey (d. 1933)
August Halm, German composer, born in Vellberg, Germany (d. 1929)
Washington Luís, President of Brazil (1926-30), born in Macaé, Rio de Janeiro (d. 1957)
Guillermo Kahlo, German-Mexican photographer and father of Frida Kahlo, born in Pforzheim, Grand Duchy of Baden, German Empire (d. 1941)
Hermann Löhr, British composer, born in Plymouth, England (d. 1943)

Thorvald Stauning, Danish tarde unionist (leader of Cigar Sorters' Union, 1896-1908), and politician (Prime Minister of Denmark, 1924-26 and 1929-42; Chairman of the Social Democratic Party, 1910-39), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1942)
Martin Lowry, English chemist (Brønsted–Lowry acid–base theory), born in Low Moor, Bradford (d. 1936)
H. B. Warner, British actor (Sunset Boulevard, The King of Kings), born in London, England (d. 1958)

Andrei Bely [Boris Nikolaevich Bugaev], Russian writer (Petersburg), born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1934)
Margaret Wycherly, British actress (Keeper of the Flame), born in London, England (d. 1956)
Napoleon Hill, American writer and philosopher (Think and Grow Rich), born in Pound, Virginia (d. 1970)
Paul Pilgrim, American athlete (Olympic gold 1904, 06), born in New York City (d. 1958)
Hermann Unger, German composer, music journalist, pedagogue and administrator (Rheinische Musikschule), born in Kamenz, Germany (d. 1958)
Mark Aldanov, Russian-French writer and critic (Novy Zhurnal), born in Kiev (d. 1957)
Nestor Makhno, Ukrainian anarchist Insurrectionary leader, born in Hulai-Pole, Russian Empire (d. 1934)
Runar Schildt, Finnish writer (Segrande Eros), born in Helsinki (d. 1925)
Miloš Crnjanski, Serbian poet (Seobe), born in Csongrád, Austria-Hungary (d. 1977)
John S Knight, American journalist and publisher (Knight Newspapers), born in Bluefield, West Virginia (d. 1981)
Beryl Rubinstein, American pianist and composer, born in Athens, Georgia (d. 1952)
(William) "Judy" Johnson, American Baseball HOF third baseman (NgL World Series 1925 Hilldale AC; NgL All-Star 1933, 1936 Pittsburgh Crawfords), born in Snow Hill, Maryland (d. 1989)
Ibrahim Abboud, 1st President and 4th Prime Minister of Sudan (1958-64), born in Suakin, Sudan (d. 1983)
Karin Boye, writer (Kallocain), born in Gothenburg, Sweden (d. 1941)
Mark Sandrich [Goldstein], American film director, writer and producer (The Gay Divorcee; Top Hat; Holiday Inn), born in New York City (d. 1945)
Beryl Markham (née Clutterbuck), British-Kenyan; racehorse trainer, aviator, and memoirist, born in Ashwell, Rutland, England (d. 1986)
Hugh Chilvers, Australian cricketer (great leggie of 30's for NSW but not Aust), born in Sawbridgeworth, Hertfordshire, England (d. 1994)
Jack Sharkey, American boxer (NYSAC, NBA and lineal heavyweight champion 1932-33), born in Binghamton, New York (d. 1994)
George Flahiff, Canadian Cardinal, born in Paris, Ontario, Canada (d. 1989)
Giovanni Salviucci, Italian composer, born in Rome, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1937)
John Krol, American former archbishop of Philadelphia (1961-88), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1996)
Sid Gillman, Pro & College Football Hall of Fame end (Ohio State U) and coach (U of Cincinnati, LA Rams, Houston Oilers), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 2003)
Sorley MacLean, Scottish poet (Poems to Eimhir and Other Poems), born in Osgaig, Raasay, Scotland (d. 1996)
Don Siegel, American film director (Escape from Alcatraz, Shootist), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1991)
Stuart J. Byrne, American sci-fi author (Star Man, God Man), born in Saint Paul, Minnesota (d. 2011)
Claude Jeter, American gospel music singer (The Swan Silvertones), born in Montgomery, Alabama (d. 2009)
Sasha Argov [Alexander Abramovich], Israeli composer, born in Moscow, Russian Empire (d. 1995)
Golfredo Corradetti, Canadian conductor and composer, born in Calgary, Alberta; (d. 1995)
Mario Biaggi, American politician (Rep-D&R-NY, 1969-88), born in New York (d. 2015)
(George) "Snuffy" Stirnweiss, American MLB baseball second baseman, 1943-52, 2X All-Star. AL Batting Title, 3X World Series (New York Yankess and 2 other teams), born in New York City (d. 1958)
Ivor Francis, Canadian-American radio, stage, and screen character actor (Dusty's Trail; The Prisoner of Second Avenue), and acting teacher, born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 1986)
Kamal El Mallakh, Egyptian archaeologist (discovered King Khufu Solar ship), and journalist, born in Upper Egypt (d. 1987)
T. C. Jones, American female impersonator, born in Scranton, Pennsylvania (d. 1971)
George Forrest, Northern Irish politician, born in Cookstown, Northern Ireland (d. 1968)
Shaw Taylor, British actor and television presenter (Police 5), born in Hackney, London, England (d. 2015)
Tex Coulter, American NFL defensive lineman (All Pro 1948, 51-52; Pro Bowl 1951-52; NY Giants), born in Fort Worth, Texas (d. 2007)
Tom Lewis, American politician (Rep-R-Florida, 1983-93), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2003)
Jan Wolkers, Dutch sculptor and novelist (Turks fruit (Turkish Delight); Kort Amerikaans (Crew Cut)), born in Oegstgeest, Netherlands (d. 2007)
Yuri Aleksandrovich Letunov, Russian cosmonaut, born in Tomsk, Russia (d. 1983)
Janet Moreau Stone, American athlete (Olympic gold 4x100m relay 1952), born in Pawtucket, Rhode Island (d. 2021)
Francisco Solano López, Argentine comic artist, born in Buenos Aires (d. 2011)
Airlene Evans, 1st American child born in a flying aircraft, born in the skies above Miami, Florida[1]
Georgette Amowitz, French choreographer and educator, born in Paris, France (d. 2008)
Hans Peter Haller, German composer, born in Radolfzell, Germany (d. 2006)
Neal Matthews, Jr., American singer (The Jordanaires), born in Nashville, Tennessee (d. 2000)
Roland Hemond, American baseball executive (GM Chicago White Sox 1970–85; Baltimore Orioles 1988–95), born in Central Falls, Rhode Island (d. 2021)
John Arden, English novelist and playwright (Left Handed Liberty), born in Barnsley, England (d. 2012)
Hank Garrett [Henry Weinblatt], American actor (Car 54 Where Are You?; Three Days of the Condor), and professional wrestler (The Minnesota Farm Boy), born in Monticello, New York
Anthony Beilenson, American politician (Rep-D-California, 1977-97), born in New York (d. 2017)
André Van Herpe, Belgian soccer midfielder who played 7 internationals; ARA La Gantoise 1952-62, born in Eine, Belgium (d. 2024)
Hans-Joachim Rödelius, German electronic and ambient music composer, keyboardist, and synthesizer player (Cluster; Harmonia), born in Berlin, Germany
Jacques Loussier, French pianist and composer who created jazz arrangements of Bach and other classical music, born in Angers, France (d. 2019)
Ulrich Plenzdorf, German writer (Die neuen Leiden des jungen W.), born in Berlin (d. 2007)
Al Casey, American rock and session guitarist (Duane Eddy; The Wrecking Crew), born in Long Beach, California (d. 2006)
Bruce Belland, American singer (Tim Conway Hour), born in Chicago, Illinois
Shelley Morrison, American actress (General Hospital, Will & Grace), born in New York City (d. 2019)
Dave Gavitt, American basketball coach, formed Basketball's Big East Conference (1979), born in Westerly, Rhode Island (d. 2011)
Bernadette Lafont, French actress (Perils of Gwendoline), born in Nimes, France (d. 2013)
Charles Stenholm, American politician (Rep-D-Texas, 1979-2005), born in Stamford, Texas (d. 2023)
Graham Serjeant, British doctor and researcher known for his work on sickle cell disease in Jamaica, born in Bristol, England (d. 2025)
Vladimir Mikhailovich Beloborodov, Russian cosmonaut, born in Oloviyanna, Chita Region, Russia
Gennadi Strekalov, Russian cosmonaut (Soyuz T-3, T-8, T-11, TM-21), born in Mytishchi, Soviet Union (d. 2004)
Mario Orozco, American 1st victim of NYC's Zodiac killer (survives), birthplace unknown
Harald Nielsen, Danish soccer forward (14 caps; Olympic silver 1960; Frederikshavn fI, Bologna FC), born in Frederikshavn, Denmark (d. 2015)
Bob Hoskins, English actor (Who Framed Roger Rabbit), born in Bury St Edmunds, West Suffolk, England (d. 2014)
Colin Loughnan, Australian rock and jazz saxophonist, vocalist, arranger, composer, and teacher (Sydney Conservatorium of Music), born in Sydney, Australia
